text: The surgeon creates a tunnel through the lesser sac along the lesser curvature of the stomach. A small incision is made at the angle of His, followed by dissection and stapling of the posterior fundus to form a gastric pouch. The omentum is then taken down.

The surgeon uses two instruments to apply tension and countertension while identifying the ligament of Treitz. A loop of intestine is formed with the biliopancreatic limb visible on the right side of the screen. The liver retractor falls, but the pouch remains stable, allowing the surgeon to sew the straight line of the pouch to the underside of the limb. The biliopancreatic limb is on the right, and the staple line is run to the back side of the loop to maintain tension.

The surgeon grabs the intestinal loop and performs a surgeon's knot, creating a two and a half centimeter anastomosis on the anti-mesenteric side. Using a fenestrated bipolar as a right angle, the surgeon mirrors the incision, ensuring a lip for sewing by positioning a centimeter off the staple line. Full thickness bites are sewn with two vicryl sutures, starting at the center and sewing around to complete the hand-sewn gastrojejunostomy.

The surgeon places the second stitch, running the left stitch to the right and the right stitch to the left, ensuring no hole remains at the site where the stitches meet. The needle is loaded and used to lock the corners, with a technique of lifting up and pushing down to set each stitch. A 40 French visagee is inserted to confirm the loop is not narrowed, and the process is repeated on the opposite side, crossing the stitches.

The surgeon secures the nest muscles using four or five interrupted Lembert sutures to relieve tension, including the outflow and inflow areas. A slip knot is created with a C loop using the limb on the screen right, tacked with a fenestrated bipolar. The surgeon switches hands to create a square knot, lifting up on the needle and sliding down the tie that exited the tissue last to maintain tension and lock the knot.

The surgeon releases tension from the muscle using lumbrics and approaches the nearest vessel. A tunnel is created underneath, and a stapler is used to staple off the tissue. The folds are traced, and several bites are taken with the muscle sealer near the root. The biliopancreatic limb appears venous congested, but maintains reasonable blood flow. The Roux limb is run to the right upper quadrant, and the jejunal loop is positioned as a candy cane, laying on the lateral side and straightening as it falls into the mid abdomen.

The surgeon uses ICD-Green to assess blood flow, observing a slow but reasonable outflow, indicating no necrosis risk. They proceed with creating a one to one and a half centimeter anastomosis for biliopancreatic fluid passage, ensuring the opening is appropriately sized for its function.

The surgeon creates a centimeter to centimeter and a half anastomosis on the elementary limb using two vicryl sutures, sewing around the site. The JJ's or GJ's anastomosis is slightly larger at twelve inches. The surgeon uses a loaded needle to control the stitching, performing a backhanded stitch to sew across the site without making it too tight. The mesenteric defect is closed, with the assistant holding the left-side loop to prevent it from folding over, allowing the surgeon to expose and sew the defect, completing the Roux-Y configuration.
